blurb: Read both sides of the street: Latin and Cyrillic

sections.heading: One language, two scripts
body: Uzbek is written in **two alphabets at once**: official **Latin** on new signs, schoolbooks and branding; **Cyrillic** on older signs, books and much of the internet. To read Uzbekistan, you need to recognise both.
The good news: they map onto each other almost letter for letter. Learn a handful of special letters and you can convert mechanically.

sections.heading: The four special Cyrillic letters
body: Four Cyrillic letters carry the special Uzbek sounds: **Ў** = o' (Ўзбекистон = O'zbekiston), **Қ** = q (Самарқанд = Samarqand), **Ғ** = g', and **Ҳ** = h.
Careful with the lookalike: plain **Х** is Latin **x** (the “kh” sound), while tailed **Ҳ** is plain h. Also handy: **Ш** = sh, **Ч** = ch, **Й** = y.

examples.english: Самарқанд in Cyrillic — tailed Қ is the deep q
ҳалол in Cyrillic — tailed Ҳ is plain h (not kh)

options.explain: Т-о-ш-к-е-н-т, letter for letter — Ш is sh.
Ш maps to sh, not ch (that would be Ч).
Close — but there's no extra i. Е here is just e.
ч = ch, о = o, й = y.
ч is ch, never a bare c.
sh would be ш — this is ч, ch.
Tailed Қ is the deep q — Uzbek keeps it distinct from k.
So close! But Қ (with the tail) maps to q, not k.
x would be Х — this is Қ, the deep q.

exercises.instruction: Which Cyrillic letter writes the gargled g' sound?

options.explain: Ғ with the bar = g'. Tails and bars mark the Uzbek specials.
Plain Г is a plain g.
Қ is the deep q — g''s voiceless cousin.

wrapUp: Latin for the new, Cyrillic for the old — and a four-letter cheat code: Ў=o', Қ=q, Ғ=g', Ҳ=h. You can now read both sides of the street.
